definition: Software as a Service — cloud-based software delivered via the internet on a subscription basis, eliminating the need for local installation.
Application Programming Interface — a set of protocols and tools that allows different software applications to communicate with each other.
Artificial Intelligence — technology that enables machines to simulate human intelligence, including learning, reasoning, and problem-solving.
Large Language Model — an AI model trained on massive amounts of text data that can understand and generate human-like text.
Generative Pre-trained Transformer — a type of large language model developed by OpenAI that generates text based on patterns learned during training.
Customer Relationship Management — software that helps businesses manage interactions with current and potential customers, tracking sales and communications.
A development approach that allows users to build applications without writing traditional programming code, using visual interfaces and drag-and-drop tools.
A development approach that requires minimal hand-coding, using visual interfaces with the option to add custom code for advanced functionality.
An automated message sent from one application to another when a specific event occurs, enabling real-time data synchronization between systems.
An open standard authorization protocol that allows third-party applications to access user data without exposing passwords.
Single Sign-On — an authentication method that allows users to log in to multiple applications with one set of credentials.
Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment — practices that automate the building, testing, and deployment of software changes.
A set of practices combining software development (Dev) and IT operations (Ops) to shorten the development lifecycle and deliver updates more frequently.
A visual project management method using boards and cards to track work items as they move through stages of a workflow.
A project management methodology emphasizing iterative development, collaboration, and flexibility to adapt to changing requirements.
An agile framework for managing work through fixed-length iterations called sprints, with defined roles and ceremonies.
A pricing model offering a free tier with basic features, with paid plans available for advanced functionality.
Annual Recurring Revenue — the total predictable revenue a subscription business expects to generate in a year.
Monthly Recurring Revenue — the predictable revenue a subscription business generates each month from active subscriptions.
The percentage of customers who cancel their subscription or stop using a service within a given time period.
Search Engine Optimization — the practice of improving a website's visibility in search engine results through content, technical, and link-building strategies.
Search Engine Results Page — the page displayed by a search engine in response to a user's query.
Click-Through Rate — the ratio of users who click on a link compared to the total number of users who view it.
The percentage of visitors who complete a desired action, such as making a purchase or signing up for a service.
A method of comparing two versions of a webpage or feature to determine which performs better based on user behavior data.
A subset of AI where systems learn and improve from data without being explicitly programmed, identifying patterns and making decisions.
A computing system inspired by biological neural networks that can learn to perform tasks by analyzing training data.
Retrieval-Augmented Generation — an AI technique that enhances LLM responses by retrieving relevant information from external knowledge sources.
A database optimized for storing and querying high-dimensional vector embeddings, used in AI similarity search and recommendation systems.
Extract, Transform, Load — a data integration process that extracts data from sources, transforms it into a usable format, and loads it into a destination system.
An architectural approach where an application is built as a collection of small, independent services that communicate over APIs.
A method of packaging software with all its dependencies into isolated units (containers) that can run consistently across environments.
A cloud execution model where the provider manages server infrastructure, allowing developers to focus on code that runs in response to events.
Content Delivery Network — a distributed network of servers that delivers web content to users based on their geographic location for faster load times.
User Experience — the overall experience a user has when interacting with a product, encompassing usability, accessibility, and satisfaction.
User Interface — the visual elements and interactive components through which users interact with a software application.
Return on Investment — a measure of the profitability of an investment, calculated as net profit divided by the cost of investment.
Key Performance Indicator — a measurable value that demonstrates how effectively a company is achieving its business objectives.
Objectives and Key Results — a goal-setting framework that defines objectives and tracks measurable key results to achieve them.
Minimum Viable Product — the most basic version of a product that includes only essential features needed to validate an idea with early users.
Daily Active Users / Monthly Active Users — metrics measuring the number of unique users who engage with a product within a given timeframe.
Net Promoter Score — a metric measuring customer loyalty by asking how likely they are to recommend a product on a 0-10 scale.
Software Development Kit — a collection of tools, libraries, and documentation that developers use to build applications for a specific platform.
Representational State Transfer API — an architectural style for building web services that use HTTP methods to access and manipulate resources.
A query language for APIs that allows clients to request exactly the data they need, reducing over-fetching and under-fetching of data.
A content management system that stores and delivers content via APIs without a built-in frontend, allowing flexibility in how content is displayed.
A web architecture using JavaScript, APIs, and Markup to build fast, secure, and scalable static websites with dynamic functionality.
The use of technology to perform tasks with minimal human intervention, connecting different tools and triggering actions based on events.
A sequence of steps or tasks that data or work items follow to complete a business process, often automated for efficiency.
The process of connecting different software systems so they can share data and work together seamlessly.
A centralized repository that stores large volumes of raw data in its native format until needed for analytics or processing.
